title: Contact
text:
M@TE accepts model submissions via GitHub. To submit your geoscientific model, open an issue in the submission repository:
👉 Model Submission Repository
Use the structured issue template to provide metadata about your model, including:

Model title, abstract, and description
Creator ORCID identifiers
Associated publication DOI
Software used
Research tags
Links to model files (code, inputs, outputs)

Submissions are currently by invitation only as M@TE is being intentional about showcasing a diverse and representative set of models.




For general enquiries about M@TE, please open a GitHub Discussion or Issue in the main repository:
👉 ModelAtlasofTheEarth/website




Join the growing community of researchers building reproducible, reusable geoscientific models. Together we make Earth science more open and accessible.

title: Welcome to Jules G Org
text: Welcome to the Jules G Org blog! This is the first post on our new Quarto- powered website, so let’s use it to explain what this place is all about.

title: Welcome to Jules G Org
text: Welcome to the Jules G Org blog! This is the first post on our new Quarto- powered website, so let’s use it to explain what this place is all about.

title: Welcome to Jules G Org
text:
What Is Jules G Org?
Jules G Org is a GitHub organisation that collects open-source projects spanning data science, tooling, and technical writing.

title: Welcome to Jules G Org
text:
How This Site Is Built
The site is generated using Quarto — an open-source scientific and technical publishing system that can render .qmd files (Markdown + optional code cells) into polished HTML websites, PDFs, and more.






flowchart LR
    A[Edit .qmd files] --> B[git push to main]
    B --> C[GitHub Actions]
    C --> D[quarto render]
    D --> E[gh-pages branch]
    E --> F[julesg-org.github.io]




Figure 1: How the site is built and deployed






Editing Content
All content lives in .qmd files at the root of the julesg-org.github.org repository:



Path
What it controls




_quarto.yml
Site-wide config (navbar, theme, footer)


index.qmd
Home / landing page


about.qmd
About page


blog/posts/<post>/index.qmd
Individual blog posts


projects/sample-project/index.qmd
Sub-project page


styles.css
Custom CSS overrides

title: Welcome to Jules G Org
text:
What’s Next?
Look out for posts on:

🐍 Python and R data science workflows
📦 Building reusable packages
🔧 DevOps and CI/CD for data projects

Stay tuned, and feel free to open an issue or pull request on GitHub!

title: News
text:
News
Stay up to date with the latest announcements, publications, and events from the M@TE community.


Coming Soon
News and blog posts will appear here as the M@TE platform grows. Check back for:

New model announcements — when new geoscientific models are added to the atlas
Community updates — workshops, conferences, and collaborations
Feature releases — improvements to the M@TE platform and tooling
Publications — peer-reviewed papers associated with M@TE models


In the meantime, follow the M@TE project on GitHub: ModelAtlasofTheEarth

title: Sample Project
text: 🏠 Jules G Org · ← Back to home · All Projects

title: Sample Project
text:
About This Project
Sample Project is a demonstration repository that shows how any repository in the julesg-org GitHub organisation can contribute a page to the main organisation website at julesg-org.github.io.
This page was generated from content inside the main website repository (julesg-org.github.org). In a real workflow, the content on this page would mirror or embed information from the sample-project repository.

title: Sample Project
text:
Features

✅ Clean, reproducible structure (Quarto + Markdown)
✅ Links back to the Jules G Org home page
✅ Integrated into the main site’s navigation and search
✅ Automatic dark / light mode support
✅ Category tags for filtering on the Projects listing

title: Sample Project
text:
Getting Started
# Clone the sample project repository
git clone https://github.com/julesg-org/sample-project.git
cd sample-project

# Install dependencies (example for a Python project)
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Run the project
python main.py

title: Sample Project
text:
Project Structure
sample-project/
├── README.md          # Overview and quick-start guide
├── main.py            # Entry point
├── src/               # Source code
│   └── analysis.py
├── data/              # Input data (not committed — see .gitignore)
├── notebooks/         # Jupyter / Quarto notebooks
└── tests/             # Unit tests

title: Sample Project
text:
Usage Example
# Example: loading and analysing data with this project
from src.analysis import load_data, summarise

df = load_data("data/sample.csv")
summary = summarise(df)
print(summary)

title: Sample Project
text:
Results & Outputs
This section would normally contain visualisations or key findings. For a real project, embed Quarto code cells here so that outputs are reproduced automatically every time the site is built:
```{python}
# Uncomment the lines below and add real code for live output
# import pandas as pd
# df = pd.read_csv("data/sample.csv")
# df.describe()
```

text:

Issues

Main site
julesg-org.github.io










TipHow to add your own project




Create a new folder in projects/ inside the main repo.
Copy this file (projects/sample-project/index.qmd) as a starting template.
Fill in your project’s title, description, and content.
Open a pull request — once merged, your project page appears automatically.





Part of Jules G Org · Built with Quarto

title: About M@TE
text:
M@TE is designed to meet geoscientists where they are: from educators and students using visualizations in the classroom to researchers developing and benchmarking new numerical models. It lowers the learning curve of numerical models and bridges the gap between traditional geology and numerical geosciences.
M@TE enables you to:

Explore and visualize models through an intuitive web interface: play animations, use them in your teaching or to better understand Earth system concepts.
Download and build on models by accessing input files and configuration scripts to reproduce or expand upon simulations on your own.
Analyze and benchmark model outputs by comparing results across models and datasets without having to recreate simulations from scratch.
Upload your own models using streamlined tools for packaging code, metadata and outputs. These are stored, preserved and assigned a DOI for citation. (Currently by invitation only, as we are being intentional about showcasing a diverse and representative set of models.)

Together we build reproducible, reusable, robust models — it takes a village — so please cite the models you use.



M@TE combines a user-friendly experience with a robust backend built on widely adopted, sustainable infrastructure. Users browse model summaries through the website, which links to GitHub and NCI repositories for downloading code and outputs. Behind the scenes, carefully chosen tools ensure consistency, accessibility and long-term preservation.
Key design choices include:


Model metadata is packaged using the RO-Crate standard, a lightweight JSON-LD format that captures rich metadata and references model components (e.g. code, inputs, outputs, publications, authors and persistent identifiers). This ensures models are machine-readable and FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable).



Models are submitted through structured GitHub issue templates. Metadata is automatically validated and enriched via GitHub Actions, which connect to external services such as Crossref, DataCite and ORCID.



This replica of the M@TE website is built with Quarto, a scientific publishing system that delivers a fast, lightweight interface for browsing models. It integrates figures, animations and interactive content to make models discoverable and usable across different audiences. (The original M@TE site uses Gatsby, a React-based static-site generator.)



Model outputs are stored and preserved through the National Computational Infrastructure (NCI) GeoNetwork. This system supports terabyte-scale data, assigns persistent DOIs and ensures discoverability through standard catalog services.





Each scientific model has its own repository in the ModelAtlasofTheEarth GitHub organisation. When a model is ready for publication, a GitHub Actions workflow automatically:

Updates the DOI in the model repository
Creates a new branch in the website repository named after the model
Copies the .website_material/ folder contents into models/<model-name>.qmd in the Quarto site
Updates the model submission issue tracker

The branch is then merged and the site rebuilds automatically — no manual file copying required.

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text: Quarto makes it straightforward to publish a polished organisation website directly from a GitHub repository. This post walks through the setup used for this very site.

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text: Quarto makes it straightforward to publish a polished organisation website directly from a GitHub repository. This post walks through the setup used for this very site.

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text:
Prerequisites

A GitHub account / organisation
Quarto CLI installed locally
Basic familiarity with Markdown

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text:
Step 1 — Create the Repository
On GitHub, create a repository named <org>.github.io (for organisations) or <username>.github.io (for personal sites). This special naming convention tells GitHub Pages to serve the repo at https://<org>.github.io.
For example:
Organisation: julesg-org
Repository:   julesg-org.github.io   ← served at https://julesg-org.github.io

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text:
Step 2 — Initialise a Quarto Website
# In your cloned repository folder:
quarto create project website .
This creates the minimal _quarto.yml and index.qmd scaffold.

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text:
Step 3 — Customise _quarto.yml
Open _quarto.yml and set at minimum:
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "Your Org Name"
  site-url: https://your-org.github.io
  navbar:
    left:
      - text: "Home"
        href: index.qmd
      - text: "About"
        href: about.qmd

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text:
Step 4 — Add GitHub Actions
Create .github/workflows/deploy.yml to automate builds on push:
name: Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2
      - run: quarto render
      - uses: peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v4
        with:
          github_token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          publish_dir: ./_site

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text:
Step 5 — Enable GitHub Pages
In your repository: Settings → Pages → Source → gh-pages branch.
Push to main and the site deploys automatically! 🎉

title: Setting Up a Quarto Website for Your GitHub Organisation
text:
Next Steps

Add blog posts under blog/posts/<post-name>/index.qmd
Add project pages under projects/<project-name>/index.qmd
Customise colours in styles.css

title: M@TE — Model Atlas of the Earth
text:
What is M@TE?
M@TE (Model Atlas of the Earth) is designed to meet geoscientists where they are: from educators and students using visualizations in the classroom to researchers developing and benchmarking new numerical models. It lowers the learning curve of numerical models and bridges the gap between traditional geology and numerical geosciences.
M@TE enables you to:

Explore and visualize models through an intuitive web interface: play animations, use them in your teaching or to better understand Earth system concepts.
Download and build on models by accessing input files and configuration scripts to reproduce or expand upon simulations on your own.
Analyze and benchmark model outputs by comparing results across models and datasets without having to recreate simulations from scratch.
Upload your own models using streamlined tools for packaging code, metadata and outputs — stored, preserved and assigned a DOI for citation.

Why M@TE?

  ✅ Open source and free
  🔎 FAIR data principles
  ♻️ Reproducible models
  🏗️ Built on sustainable infrastructure
  📦 RO-Crate packaging
  🆔 DOI-assigned datasets

Together we build reproducible, reusable, robust models — it takes a village — so please cite the models you use.

title: Constraining the response of continental-scale groundwater flow to climate change
text:
Constraining the response of continental-scale groundwater flow to climate change

  
    DOI:
    
      DOI
      10.25914/t2mn-bk81
    
    
    Creators:
    Ben Mather
    Dietmar Müller
    Craig O'Neill
    Adam Beall
    R.Willem Vervoort
    Louis-Noel Moresi
    
    Tags:
    groundwater
    thermal-hydraulic
    Bayesian
    water-management
    Python
  

  
  
    
  
  
    Coupled heat-groundwater flow model of the Sydney–Gunnedah–Bowen Basin based on the MAP estimate of material properties and boundary conditions.
  

  
  Abstract
  
    Numerical models of groundwater flow play a critical role for water management scenarios under climate extremes.
    Large-scale models play a key role in determining long range flow pathways from continental interiors to the
    oceans, yet struggle to simulate the local flow patterns offered by small-scale models. We have developed a
    highly scalable numerical framework to model continental groundwater flow which captures the intricate flow
    pathways between deep aquifers and the near-surface. The coupled thermal-hydraulic basin structure is inferred
    from hydraulic head measurements, recharge estimates from geochemical proxies, and borehole temperature data
    using a Bayesian framework. We use it to model the deep groundwater flow beneath the Sydney–Gunnedah–Bowen
    Basin, part of Australia's largest aquifer system. Coastal aquifers have flow rates of up to 0.3 m/day, and a
    corresponding groundwater residence time of just 2,000 years. In contrast, our model predicts slow flow rates
    of 0.005 m/day for inland aquifers, resulting in a groundwater residence time of 400,000 years. Perturbing the
    model to account for a drop in borehole water levels since 2000, we find that lengthened inland flow pathways
    depart significantly from pre-2000 streamlines as groundwater is drawn further from recharge zones in a drying
    climate. Our results illustrate that progressively increasing water extraction from inland aquifers may
    permanently alter long-range flow pathways. Our open-source modelling approach can be extended to any basin
    and may help inform policies on the sustainable management of groundwater.
  

  
  Description
  
    This model was developed in order to study groundwater flow on a continental scale, focusing on the
    Sydney–Gunnedah–Bowen Basin in Australia. Using data such as hydraulic head measurements and borehole
    temperatures, it predicts how water moves through deep aquifers to the surface. Coastal aquifers show fast
    water flow, while inland aquifers have much slower flow. The study shows that increased water extraction
    from inland areas could permanently change water flow patterns. This open-source model can be used for other
    regions and aims to support sustainable groundwater management policies.
  

  
  Model Setup
  
  
    3D stratigraphy of the Sydney–Gunnedah–Bowen Basin. The vertical spacing of layers has been exaggerated for visual clarity.
  
  
    We apply our numerical framework to the Sydney–Gunnedah–Bowen (SGB) Basin in eastern Australia. The SGB
    Basin covers about 1.5 million square kilometers, and we model it in high-resolution 3D using over 10 million
    cells (6 × 6 × 0.6 km, in the x, y, z directions, respectively) to detail flow patterns down to 12 km
    beneath the crust. By adjusting the model to match real-world data, it provides accurate insights into water
    and heat movement through deep aquifers in large areas. Temperature advection due to groundwater flow is
    described by the advection-diffusion equation. Darcy flux is calculated from the groundwater flow equation.
    Groundwater recharge and discharge are driven by changes in hydraulic head, set to the height of the water
    table at the top boundary surface.
  

  
  Software
  
    Underworld2: Python Geodynamics Modelling for Desktop, HPC and Cloud
    https://github.com/underworldcode/underworld2
    DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.7455999
  

  
  Compute Tags
  
    Python
    C
    finite element
    heat equation
    advection-diffusion
  

  
  Dataset
  
    Model files and outputs are available via NCI (National Computational Infrastructure):
    
      https://thredds.nci.org.au/thredds/catalog/nm08/MATE/mather-2022-groundwater/catalog.html
    
  
  
    Model scripts are also available on GitHub:
    
      https://github.com/brmather/Sydney_Basin/tree/master
    
  
  
    The model output data contains: .h5 (Underworld2 data files), .xdmf (Underworld2 xdmf header files),
    .csv, .npz, .png, .pvsm (Paraview state files), .txt.
  

  
  Associated Publication
  
    Mather, B., Müller, R.D., O'Neill, C., Beall, A., Vervoort, R.W., & Moresi, L. (2022).
    Constraining the response of continental-scale groundwater flow to climate change.
    Scientific Reports, Springer Nature.
    DOI: 10.1038/s41598-022-08384-w
  

  
  Licence
  
    
      Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)
    
  

  
  Citation
  
    Mather, B., Müller, R.D., O'Neill, C., Beall, A., Vervoort, R.W., & Moresi, L. (2024).
    Constraining the response of continental-scale groundwater flow to climate change [Data set].
    AuScope, National Computational Infrastructure.
    https://doi.org/10.25914/t2mn-bk81
  

  
  Funders
  
    NSW Department of Industry
    AuScope — https://ror.org/04s1m4564
